title: String Formatting (Style Guide)
content:
### Comparing Formatting Styles

Python offers **multiple ways** to format strings. This guide helps you choose.

---

### Style Comparison

| Method | Syntax | When to Use |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| **f-strings** | `f"Hello {name}"` | Python 3.6+ (preferred) |
| **`.format()`** | `"Hello {}".format(name)` | Dynamic format strings |
| **`%`-formatting** | `"Hello %s" % name` | Legacy code only |
| **`+` concatenation** | `"Hello " + name` | Simple cases |
| **`Template`** | `Template("$name")` | User-provided templates |

---

### f-Strings (Best Choice)

```python
name = "Alice"
age = 25
print(f"{name} is {age}")  # Clean and fast
```

### .format() (When Dynamic)

```python
template = "My name is {name}"
result = template.format(name="Bob")
```

### %-Formatting (Legacy)

```python
print("Name: %s, Age: %d" % (name, age))
```

---

### Performance Comparison

| Method | Speed | Readability | Modern |
|--------|-------|-------------|--------|
| f-strings | Fastest | Best | Yes |
| `.format()` | Medium | Good | Yes |
| `%` | Fast | Poor | No |
| `+` | Slow | Poor | N/A |

---

> **Recommendation:** Use f-strings by default. Only use `.format()` when you need dynamic templates. Avoid `%`-formatting in new code.
codeExample:
name = "Alice"
age = 25

# f-strings (preferred)
print(f"{name} is {age} years old")

# .format()
print("{} is {} years old".format(name, age))

# %-formatting
print("%s is %d years old" % (name, age))

quiz.question: Which is the recommended string formatting method for new Python code?
